item.title: The perilously high stakes for the next UN leader
item.description: International body must sharpen its focus to stay relevant in a fractured world

item.title: African economies are more resilient than ever
item.description: The continent is better positioned to weather crises, yet perceptions remain stuck in the past

item.title: The US decoupling from the global gloom has limits
item.description: Total economic and financial insulation is an impossibility

item.title: Our pension funds are not quasi-government money
item.description: New legislation pushing the mandatory allocation of investments into private assets conflicts with the fiduciary duty of trustees

item.title: AI + Halo = $$$
item.description: A techno-industrial boomlet

item.title: Trouble for Labour across a scrambled political map
item.description: Breadth of previous support for the party makes it vulnerable to losing everywhere, to everyone, this week

item.title: America’s retail army now rules the stock market
item.description: Unbowed by the Iran war and other shocks, individual investors are buying their way to new heights of influence

item.title: Can gardens really save the world?
item.description: The climate zeitgeist is turning to backyards, but better ratings and rewards are needed to convince us all of their true power

item.title: The land of milk and no money: UK farmers are in a fix
item.description: Prices are falling due to a glut, yet the costs of feed, fertilisers and fuel are rising

item.title: Trump’s strategy of accommodation with China is a historic mistake
item.description: The upcoming summit in Beijing could lock in an American posture of strategic deference

item.title: Lebanon needs help to secure its economic recovery
item.description: The international community must support a reform-driven government now or risk a far more destabilised reality

item.title: America’s political identity crisis
item.description: The outer edges of both parties are overlapping on a growing number of issues

item.title: There is method in the market’s madness
item.description: But it relies on a host of rosy assumptions going in investors’ favour

item.title: Trump’s green new deal
item.description: The US president’s actions have inadvertently raised the appeal of renewable energy

item.title: Why co-CEOs might suit our tumultuous times
item.description: Sharing the leadership burden remains rare but is not without benefits

item.title: Vinted challenges the threadbare finances of second-hand fashion
item.description: The company is now worth more than twice as much as it was in 2021

item.title: EM’s vanishing diversification
item.description: AI concentration makes EMs look like DMs

item.title: The physical world strikes back
item.description: The Iran war is a reminder that geographic facts rather than digital tech shape our lives

item.title: The problem with healthy life expectancy
item.description: A crude blend of very different statistics is not the best tool for the job

item.title: Meta stock might look cheap if it weren’t for Mark Zuckerberg
item.description: Not only is the Facebook boss spinning a lot of plates, but his worldview is not like that of a typical chief executive

item.title: Theatre investing could benefit from a venture capital mindset
item.description: But an enticing backdrop can’t hide the fact that the risks are real and sizeable

item.title: The euro’s urgent need
item.description: Europe must finally grasp the opportunity to develop the single currency as a reserve asset

item.title: Paranoid parenting in the age of AI
item.description: It’s an illusion to think we can robot-proof our kids’ education choices

item.title: The luxuries I can’t give up (even as prices rise)
item.description: I know it’s ridiculous to pay £32 for shower gel, but not all luxuries are frivolous

item.title: Sentosa, London: An essential Singapore chilli crab pilgrimage — restaurant review
item.description: Eat crab. Get messy. Don’t think about the dry cleaning bill
